model.label: Claude Opus 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: BYU holds a meaningful edge in predictive metrics, ranking 23rd in KenPom with an adjusted efficiency margin of +23.28 compared to NC State's approximate +14-15, driven by one of the nation's best offenses (AdjO 125.5, 10th nationally) that should exploit NC State's glaring defensive weakness (DRtg 108.8, 226th nationally). The Cougars are led by AP First-Team All-American AJ Dybantsa, a generational freshman talent capable of taking over games, while NC State enters having lost seven of their last nine and already fatigued from a play-in game. While NC State's ACC pedigree and tournament experience give them a credible upset path in this historically competitive 6/11 seed line, BYU's roughly 8-point efficiency margin advantage and offensive firepower make them the clear favorite.

reasoningStep.title: BYU vs NC State – West 6/11 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: BYU's elite offense and superior overall efficiency profile, anchored by projected top pick AJ Dybantsa, should overwhelm NC State's poor defense. The Wolfpack's late-season slide and play-in game fatigue further tilt this matchup toward the Cougars.
evidence: BYU KenPom #23 (AdjO 125.5/10th, AdjD 102.3, AdjEM +23.28) vs NC State ~KenPom #34 with DRtg 108.8 (226th nationally)
BYU Torvik #33 (AdjOE 124.8, AdjDE 104.3) vs NC State Torvik ~#38
NC State record 20-13 with SRS 18.02 (31st) vs BYU 23-11 with stronger efficiency margins
NC State lost 7 of last 9 games entering the tournament and played a play-in game adding fatigue
BYU led by AP First-Team All-American AJ Dybantsa, a projected top NBA draft pick
Historical 6/11 upset rate ~40%, but BYU's quality gap is wider than typical for this seed line

result.text: BYU's NCAA Tournament draw is fairly favorable for Cougars – Deseret News Deseret News Deseret Magazine Church News Deseret News Deseret Magazine Print Subscriptions Newsletter Sign Up Latest Latest Politics Politics Utah Utah Sports Sports Opinion Opinion Magazine Magazine More More Sign In Register Sign In Search Go Sports BYU Cougars Analysis: BYU is dancing in the NCAA Tournament, which is ‘all that really matters’ after adversity filled season While Cougars’ much-anticipated 2025-26 campaign was a bit disappointing, coach Kevin Young’s squad gets a fresh start in Portland Published: March 15, 2026, 7:19 p.m. MDT View 80 Comments Share BYU players celebrate in the Marriott Center Annex in Provo as their name was called during the NCAA Tournament selection show on Sunday, March 15, 2026. Courtesy BYU Athletics By Jay Drew Jay has covered sports in Utah for more than 30 years and has been writing for the Deseret News since 2019. Your browser does not support the audio element. Play audio NEW: Try Article Audio NEW: Try Article Audio Audio quality: | Skip back 15 seconds Play audio Skip forward 15 seconds 00:00 00:00 Decrease playback rate 1.0 x Increase playback rate 00:00 / 00:00 Skip back 15 seconds Play audio Skip forward 15 seconds - 0.5x 0.6x 0.7x 0.8x 0.9x 1.0x 1.1x 1.2x 1.3x 1.4x 1.5x 1.6x 1.7x 1.8x 1.9x 2.0x 2.1x 2.2x 2.3x 2.4x 2.5x 2.6x 2.7x 2.8x 2.9x 3.0x + Perhaps superstar freshman AJ Dybantsa said it best after the NCAA Tournament bracket was revealed Sunday afternoon and the up-and-down BYU Cougars found themselves as a No. 6 seed playing in Portland, Oregon. “Obviously going into the tournament you wanted to be a higher seed, but as long as you’re dancing, that’s all that really matters,” Dybantsa said in a teleconference with reporters from the Marriott Center Annex. “We had a lot of adversity through this year with injuries and everything like that, so for us to even be a six seed … we’re proud of that.” In other words, it could have been worse. A couple weeks ago after three straight losses, including one at home to so-so UCF and two to teams that didn’t make the tournament — West Virginia and Cincinnati — the Cougars would have done just about anything for a No. 6 seed. The fact that they get to start the tournament out West, where BYU fans will be plentiful at the Moda Center — much like they were last year in Denver — is icing on the cake. Sure, a possible second-round game against former WCC foe Gonzaga, a No. 3 seed, could be problematic with Spokane a half-day’s drive (5-6 hours) away from Portland, but first things first. The Cougars will have to get past either Texas or North Carolina State, as those power conference teams were among the Last Four In and will meet on Tuesday in Dayton, Ohio, for the right to move on and face BYU (23-11). Later Sunday, the NCAA announced that BYU’s game Thursday will tip off at approximately 5:25 p.m. MDT in Portland and will be televised by TBS. Brad Nessler, Wally Szczerbiak and

BYU's NCAA Tournament draw is fairly favorable for Cougars – Deseret News Deseret News Deseret Magazine Church News Deseret News Deseret Magazine Print Subscriptions Newsletter Sign Up Latest Latest Politics Politics Utah Utah Sports Sports Opinion Opinion Magazine Magazine More More Sign In Register Sign In Search Go Sports BYU Cougars Analysis: BYU is dancing in the NCAA Tournament, which is ‘all that really matters’ after adversity filled season While Cougars’ much-anticipated 2025-26 campaign was a bit disappointing, coach Kevin Young’s squad gets a fresh start in Portland Published: March 15, 2026, 7:19 p.m. MDT View 80 Comments Share BYU players celebrate in the Marriott Center Annex in Provo as their name was called during the NCAA Tournament selection show on Sunday, March 15, 2026. Courtesy BYU Athletics By Jay Drew Jay has covered sports in Utah for more than 30 years and has been writing for the Deseret News since 2019. Your browser does not support the audio element. Play audio NEW: Try Article Audio NEW: Try Article Audio Audio quality: | Skip back 15 seconds Play audio Skip forward 15 seconds 00:00 00:00 Decrease playback rate 1.0 x Increase playback rate 00:00 / 00:00 Skip back 15 seconds Play audio Skip forward 15 seconds - 0.5x 0.6x 0.7x 0.8x 0.9x 1.0x 1.1x 1.2x 1.3x 1.4x 1.5x 1.6x 1.7x 1.8x 1.9x 2.0x 2.1x 2.2x 2.3x 2.4x 2.5x 2.6x 2.7x 2.8x 2.9x 3.0x + Perhaps superstar freshman AJ Dybantsa said it best after the NCAA Tournament bracket was revealed Sunday afternoon and the up-and-down BYU Cougars found themselves as a No. 6 seed playing in Portland, Oregon. “Obviously going into the tournament you wanted to be a higher seed, but as long as you’re dancing, that’s all that really matters,” Dybantsa said in a teleconference with reporters from the Marriott Center Annex. “We had a lot of adversity through this year with injuries and everything like that, so for us to even be a six seed … we’re proud of that.” In other words, it could have been worse. A couple weeks ago after three straight losses, including one at home to so-so UCF and two to teams that didn’t make the tournament — West Virginia and Cincinnati — the Cougars would have done just about anything for a No. 6 seed. The fact that they get to start the tournament out West, where BYU fans will be plentiful at the Moda Center — much like they were last year in Denver — is icing on the cake. Sure, a possible second-round game against former WCC foe Gonzaga, a No. 3 seed, could be problematic with Spokane a half-day’s drive (5-6 hours) away from Portland, but first things first. The Cougars will have to get past either Texas or North Carolina State, as those power conference teams were among the Last Four In and will meet on Tuesday in Dayton, Ohio, for the right to move on and face BYU (23-11). Later Sunday, the NCAA announced that BYU’s game Thursday will tip off at approximately 5:25 p.m. MDT in Portland and will be televised by TBS. Brad Nessler, Wally Szczerbiak and

modelTrace.content:
Now I have a solid picture. Let me compile what I know:

**BYU (6-seed):**
- Record: 23-11
- KenPom: #23 (AdjO 125.5, AdjD 102.3, AdjEM +23.28)
- Torvik: #33 (AdjOE 124.8, AdjDE 104.3)
- Conference: Big 12
- Led by AJ Dybantsa (AP First-Team All-American, projected top NBA pick)
- Elite offense (10th-16th nationally)
- Described as "up-and-down" / "adversity-filled season" but still a 6-seed

**NC State (11-seed, won play-in):**
- Record: 20-13 (10-8 ACC, 7th)
- SRS: 18.02 (31st), SOS: 10.83 (32nd)
- ORtg: 119.0 (28th), DRtg: 108.8 (226th)
- From prior pick: KenPom ~#34, Torvik ~#38
- First-year coach Will Wade
- Lost 7 of last 9 entering tournament
- Already played a play-in game (fatigue factor)
- Defense is a major weakness (226th nationally in DRtg per Sports Reference)

BYU has clear advantages: better overall ratings, elite offense, a generational talent in Dybantsa, and they're rested while NC State had to play a play-in game. NC State's defense (226th in DRtg) is a massive liability against BYU's top-10 offense. The 6/11 line historically sees upsets ~40% of the time, but BYU's statistical edge is wider than typical for this seed line.

The Cougars are led by AP First-Team All-American AJ Dybantsa, a generational freshman talent capable of taking over games, while NC State enters having lost seven of their last nine and already fatigued from a play-in game.
